content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration        •••        Executive Legal        •••        Enforcement        •••        Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                                                    322-5901                            322-6441                            322-6444

November 23, 1977

77-11-077

Mr. J. Boyd Morse
3120 Wilkins Way
Carmichael, California 95608

Dear Mr. Morse:

     I have been asked to respond to your questions concerning
the impact of the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act on your newly elected position.  The facts as
we understand them are as follows.

     You are a captain with the Sacramento Fire Department,
and a member of Firefighters Local #522.  Firefighters Local
#522 is the governing union for most, if not all, fire departments in the surrounding Sacramento County area, including the
Carmichael Fire Protection District.

     It is our understanding that you were recently elected as
a director for the Carmichael Fire Protection District, and
that the directors are responsible for negotiating contracts
with Firefighters Local #522.

     The question you have asked us to consider is whether it
is a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act to
participate in matters that would involve Firefighters Local
#522.

                                                                    1/
     Section 87100 of the Government Code provides that:

          No public official at any level of state
          or local government shall make, participate
          in making or in any way attempt to use his
          official position to influence a governmental
          decision in which he knows or has reason to
          know he has a financial interest.

_____________________________
1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise indicated.

J. Boyd Morse
November 23, 1977                                                Page Two


     Section 87103(c) states that:

          An official has a financial interest in
     a decision within the meaning of Section
     87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that
     the decision will have a material financial
     effect, distinguishable from its effect on
     the public generally, on:
                    ....
          (c) Any source of income, other than loans
     by a commercial lending institution in the
     regular course of business, aggregating two
     hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
     received by or promised to the public official
     within twelve months prior to the time when
     the decision is made;....


     It is our understanding that you do not receive any income
from Firefighters Local #522. So long as you do not receive
$250 or more from the union in salary, reimbursement for
expenses and similar benefits, you need not disqualify yourself
under the Act from participating in matters involving Firefighters
Local #522.

     I hope this information has been helpful. If you have any
question, please do not hesitate to contact either me or Dwight
Dickerson.

                                                  Sincerely,

                                                  Ted Prim, Chief
                                                  Conflicts of Interest Division


TP:qb

Nov 13 O 33 IN 11
November 11, 1977

Mr. Bob Stern
Fair Political Practice Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Stern:

As per our phone conversation of November 7, regarding
conflict of interest in my newly elected position (effective
November 15, 1977) as Director for the Carmichael Fire Protection
District, it is essential to know exactly what role I am entitled,
lawfully and morally, to assume.

My position as Captain with the Sacramento Fire Department
encompasses, at my option, being a member of Firefighter Local
#522. Firefighter Local #522 is the contracting union for most
(if not all) fire departments in the surrounding Sacramento
County area including the Carmichael Fire Protection District.

As the newly elected Director and one of a five member board
is there any conflict of interest in carrying out my responsibili-
ties to the taxpayers, example, vote on a contract for the
department personnel in the u.F.F., who are members of local #522.

For your information I am enclosing a copy from Legislative
Counsel of California dated October 5, 1977, regarding public
employees political activities #15447, received from Local #522
on November 7, 1977.

As we discussed on November 7, Mr. Joseph Hallefer, Deputy
County Counsel, has also been approached to render a decision on
the above matter and a letter similar to this will be forwarded
to Mr. Halleffe .

Please advise at your earliest convenience.

Thank you for an consideration regarding the above request.

Sincerely,

J. Lloyd Morse
3120 Wilkins ay
tlm                 Carmichael, CA  95608
Enclosure

LEGISLATIVE COUNSEL
OF CALIFORNIA

BION M. GREGORY

RECEIVED OCT - 6 1977

Sacramento, California
October 5, 1977

Honorable Vic Fazio
5126 State Capitol

Public Employees: Political
Activities - #15447

Dear Mr. Fazio:

QUESTION

Several union members of Sacramento Area Fire
Fighters Local No. 522 who are employed as fire suppression personnel by the City of Sacramento wish to seek
election to the board of directors of fire districts in
which they reside. Local 522 is also the recognized
bargaining unit for fire suppression personnel employed
by the districts in question. In addition, the City of
Sacramento and the fire districts in question provide
mutual assistance when needed.

If the City of Sacramento firemen are elected
to the governing boards of the fire districts in question,
would a conflict of interest be involved?

OPINION

We find no conflict of interest in the above
situation.

Honorable Vic Fazio - p. 2 - #15447


ANALYSIS

     The doctrine which prohibits the holding of
incompatible offices rests in the rationale that two
offices cannot be held by one person where, from the
potentially antagonistic character of the offices,
the public interest may suffer (see Neigel v. Superior
Court, San Bernardino County, 140 Cal. Rptr. 113, 116).

     Without deciding whether a city fireman holds a
"public office," we find no incompatibility between employment
as a city fireman and as a member of the governing board of
a fire district, even though the same union will represent
both the city and district firemen.

     In determining whether two offices are incompatible,
the court, in People ex rel. Chapman v. Rapsey (16 Cal. 2d
636) quoted, with approval, 46 Corpus Juris 941, as follows:

     "'At common law the holding of one office
does not of itself disqualify the incumbent
from holding another office at the same time,
provided there is no inconsistency in the func-
tions of the two offices in question. But
where the functions of two offices are incon-
sistent, they are regarded as incompatible.
The inconsistency, which at common law makes
offices incompatible, does not consist of the
physical impossibility to discharge the duties
of both offices, but lies rather in a conflict of
interest, as where one is subordinate to the
other and subject in some degree to the super-
visory power of its incumbent, or where the
incumbent of one of the offices has the power
to remove the incumbent of the other or to
audit the accounts of the other.'" (p. 642.)

     The court continued, quoting with approval, State
v. Jones (Wis., 119 N.W. 431) as follows:

     "'It is not an essential element of incom-
patibility at common law that the clash of duty
should exist in all or in the greater part of
the official functions. If one office is superior to the other in some of its principal or im-
portant duties, so that the exercise of such
duties might conflict, to the public detriment,
with the exercise of other important duties in the
subordinate office, then the offices are in-
compatible.'" (p. 642.)

Honorable Vic Fazio - p. 3 - #15447

     It appears that, as a general proposition, the
membership on the governing board of a fire district and
employment with a city fire department would not be in-
compatible under the above rules, as neither position is
subordinate to the other, nor does either have any degree of
supervisory power over the other. A mutual assistance
agreement between the city and the district may give rise to
a conflict of interest situation, but this fact standing
alone is not enough, in our opinion, to render the two
positions incompatible.

     As regards the conflict of loyalties presented by
the employee's membership in an organization which will
bargain with him in his capacity as a fire district board
member, we find no statute or rule of law which would pro-
hibit such an arrangement. While undoubtedly the board
member's loyalties to the district's taxpayers may be tested
against his organizational loyalties in such a situation,
this is not the kind of situation which has, that we are
aware of, as yet been addressed by the Legislature or the
courts. We note that we are not here presented with the
case of a member of a district board voting upon his own
contract of employment with the district (see Sec. 1090,
Gov. C.) Nor have facts been presented to indicate that a
wage settlement negotiated by Local 522 with the fire dis-
tricts involved would affect the financial interests of City
of Sacramento fire suppression personnel.

     In conclusion, we find no conflict of interest in
this situation.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        Bion M. Gregory
                                        Legislative Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By
                                        John Corzine
                                        Deputy Legislative Counsel

JC:at

sections.analysis:
The doctrine which prohibits the holding of incompatible offices rests in the rationale that two offices cannot be held by one person where, from the potentially antagonistic character of the offices, the public interest may suffer (see Neigel v. Superior Court, San Bernardino County, 140 Cal. Rptr. 113, 116).

Without deciding whether a city fireman holds a "public office," we find no incompatibility between employment as a city fireman and as a member of the governing board of a fire district, even though the same union will represent both the city and district firemen.

In determining whether two offices are incompatible, the court, in People ex rel. Chapman v. Rapsey (16 Cal. 2d 636) quoted, with approval, 46 Corpus Juris 941, as follows:

"'At common law the holding of one office does not of itself disqualify the incumbent from holding another office at the same time, provided there is no inconsistency in the functions of the two offices in question. But where the functions of two offices are inconsistent, they are regarded as incompatible. The inconsistency, which at common law makes offices incompatible, does not consist of the physical impossibility to discharge the duties of both offices, but lies rather in a conflict of interest, as where one is subordinate to the other and subject in some degree to the supervisory power of its incumbent, or where the incumbent of one of the offices has the power to remove the incumbent of the other or to audit the accounts of the other." (p. 642.)

The court continued, quoting with approval, State v. Jones (Wis., 110 N.W. 431) as follows:

"'It is not an essential element of incompatibility at common law that the clash of duty should exist in all or in the greater part of the official functions. If one office is superior to the other in some of its principal or important duties, so that the exercise of such duties might conflict, to the public detriment, with the exercise of other important duties in the subordinate office, then the offices are incompatible." (p. 642.)

It appears that, as a general proposition, the membership on the governing board of a fire district and employment with a city fire department would not be incompatible under the above rules, as neither position is subordinate to the other, nor does either have any degree of supervisory power over the other. A mutual assistance agreement between the city and the district may give rise to a conflict of interest situation, but this fact standing alone is not enough, in our opinion, to render the two positions incompatible.

As regards the conflict of loyalties presented by the employee's membership in an organization which will bargain with him in his capacity as a fire district board member, we find no statute or rule of law which would prohibit such an arrangement. While undoubtedly the board member's loyalties to the district's taxpayers may be tested against his organizational loyalties in such a situation, this is not the kind of situation which has, that we are aware of, as yet been addressed by the Legislature or the courts. We note that we are not here presented with the case of a member of a district board voting upon his own contract of employment with the district (see Sec. 1090, Gov. C.) nor have facts been presented to indicate that a wage settlement negotiated by Local 522 with the fire districts involved would affect the financial interests of City of Sacramento fire suppression personnel.

In conclusion, we find no conflict of interest in this situation.
